plain_language_label: Sense of being abandoned by the people who should protect us
what_it_captures: Anger, disappointment, and frustration aimed at one's own state, allies, and mainstream media for failing to protect citizens' interests.
sample_item_1: In general, I distrust our international allies.
sample_item_2: I feel disappointed by the actions of the international allies in protecting my country.
theoretical_heritage: Institutional trust & political grievance (OECD, 2017; Akkerman et al., 2014)

plain_language_label: Sense of being abandoned — long form
what_it_captures: S4 long-form ABAND block (eight indicators): same construct as ABAND, extended for invariance estimation.
sample_item_1: In general, I distrust our international allies.
sample_item_2: I feel disappointed by the actions of the international allies in protecting my country.
theoretical_heritage: Institutional trust & political grievance (OECD, 2017; Akkerman et al., 2014)

plain_language_label: Anxiety-driven openness to making peace at any cost
what_it_captures: Tension and anxiety about conflict with Russia / China that translates into willingness to accommodate those powers rather than confront them.
sample_item_1: I feel tense when thinking about potential conflicts with countries like Russia or China.
sample_item_2: I would feel safer if peace with countries like Russia is struck at any cost.
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

plain_language_label: Overall affective stance toward national protection and rival powers
what_it_captures: Cross-cutting affective items used as the global DisInformeter anchor in S4 / S5; primarily a trust / sympathy index toward Russia / China and one's own state's external protection.
sample_item_1: I have a very negative feeling about how my state handles external threats.
sample_item_2: I dislike the actions of our international allies in ensuring the safety of my state.
theoretical_heritage: Composite affective anchor (DisInforMeter; see report §The Instrument)

plain_language_label: Conflict-avoidance accommodation toward Russia or China
what_it_captures: Composite of cognitive pragmatism toward Russia and China — the cross-wave umbrella label used for FEAR before the S5 affective separation.
sample_item_1: I feel tense when thinking about potential conflicts with countries like Russia or China.
sample_item_2: I would feel safer if peace with countries like Russia or China is struck at any cost
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

what_it_captures: S4 long-form PRAG block: cross-wave PRAG construct in the eight-indicator parameterisation for invariance estimation.
sample_item_1: I feel tense when thinking about potential conflicts with countries like Russia or China.
sample_item_2: I would feel safer if peace with countries like Russia or China is struck at any cost
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

what_it_captures: Affective respect, hope, and inspiration directed at Russia as an alternative civilisational model (S2 / S3 fru* items).
sample_item_1: To a certain extent, I admire Russia.
sample_item_2: Sometimes, I feel inspired by the actions of Russia.

what_it_captures: S5 pooled affective-admiration block combining the Russia and China items into one composite.
sample_item_1: To a certain extent, I admire China.
sample_item_2: Sometimes, I feel inspired by the actions of China.

plain_language_label: Felt threat to our way of life
what_it_captures: Anger, worry, sadness, and disgust at perceived demographic, cultural, and value-erosion menace to the in-group.
sample_item_1: I’m worried that our ways of life are sought to be destroyed.
sample_item_2: I am sad because our culture, language, and identity are disappearing.

what_it_captures: S5 facet of affective threat focused on perceived foreign-power encroachment / exploitation.
sample_item_1: I’m worried that my country is being exploited by foreign countries.
sample_item_2: I am angry when I think about how my country is being used by foreign countries.

what_it_captures: S5 facet of affective threat focused on perceived LGBT+ rights / visibility menace.
sample_item_1: I am worried about the spread of LGBT+ propaganda.
sample_item_2: I’m outraged when thinking about the promotion LGBT+ ideas.

plain_language_label: Felt threat to our way of life — long form
what_it_captures: S4 long-form THREAT block (eight indicators): same construct as THREAT, extended to support metric-invariance estimation.
sample_item_1: I’m worried that our ways of life are sought to be destroyed.
sample_item_2: I’m worried that my country is being exploited by foreign countries.

what_it_captures: S5 facet of affective threat focused on immigration-driven cultural change.
sample_item_1: I am angry because immigration is changing our way of life for the worse.
sample_item_2: I feel worried because of uncontrolled immigration.

plain_language_label: Distrust of NATO / EU / international allies
what_it_captures: Belief that the EU, NATO, or other international allies will not defend the nation or are weak / unreliable.
sample_item_1: EU is weak and has no power.
sample_item_2: NATO will not be brave enough to defend our nation in case of military conflict.
sample_item_source: item_mapping_overview / Unprotected by international allies / S3

plain_language_label: Sense that the state has abandoned its citizens
what_it_captures: Belief that the home state has failed in its protective and provisioning duties.
sample_item_1: My state has abandoned its citizens.
sample_item_2: My state is exploiting its citizens.
sample_item_source: item_mapping_overview / Abandoned by own state / S3
theoretical_heritage: Institutional trust & external efficacy (OECD, 2017; Niemi et al., 1991)

plain_language_label: Belief that mainstream media censor truth
what_it_captures: Belief that domestic state or mainstream media restrict access to truthful information / propagate lies.
sample_item_1: My state is censoring access to truthful information.
sample_item_2: My state is supporting media you cannot trust.

display_label: Abandoned by own state (S5 legacy label)
plain_language_label: Sense that the state has abandoned us — S5 wording
what_it_captures: S5 precursor of ABANH; retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: My state has abandoned its citizens.
sample_item_2: My state is exploiting its citizens.
sample_item_source: item_mapping_overview / Abandoned by own state / S5
theoretical_heritage: Institutional trust & external efficacy (OECD, 2017; Niemi et al., 1991)

plain_language_label: Anxiety / pragmatism toward China — S1 wording
what_it_captures: S1 precursor of PRAGCH; retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: It is a mistake that we are not allowed to collaborate with states such as China.
sample_item_2: Taiwan should finally stop provoking China.
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

plain_language_label: Anxiety / pragmatism toward Russia — S1 wording
what_it_captures: S1 precursor of PRAGR; retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: It is a mistake that we are not allowed to collaborate with such states as Russia.
sample_item_2: Countries like Ukraine should finally start building good relations with Russia.
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

plain_language_label: Belief that mainstream media censor truth — S5 wording
what_it_captures: S5 precursor of ABANM; retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: My state is censoring access to truthful information.
sample_item_2: My state is supporting media you cannot trust.

what_it_captures: Belief that the in-group's interests should override outsiders' by any means, often paired with anti-EU / anti-NATO suspicion.
sample_item_1: Our national interests are more important than other countries' interests.
sample_item_2: We should protect our nation's interests by any means necessary.

plain_language_label: Belief that foreign powers exploit our nation
what_it_captures: Conviction that foreign countries or Western institutions extract value, resources, or sovereignty from the in-group.
sample_item_1: We are unable to use our nation’s greatest potential because foreign countries are exploiting us.
sample_item_2: Foreign countries are exploiting our nation’s resources.
theoretical_heritage: Perceived foreign exploitation; realistic intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

what_it_captures: Belief that the LGBT+ movement is a threat to traditional values and that LGBT+ rights advocacy is a foreign imposition.
sample_item_1: We should protect and enforce traditional gender roles.
sample_item_2: The LGBT+ movement is a threat to our values.
theoretical_heritage: Symbolic intergroup threat from LGBT+ rights (Stephan et al., 2009)

plain_language_label: Sense of being left behind — S1 wording
what_it_captures: S1 precursor mapped to ABAND in the canonical higher-order vocabulary.
sample_item_1: My state has abandoned its citizens.
sample_item_2: My state is exploiting its citizens.
sample_item_source: item_mapping_overview / Abandoned by own state / S1
theoretical_heritage: Institutional trust & political grievance (OECD, 2017; Akkerman et al., 2014)

display_label: Abandoned by own state (S1 legacy label)
plain_language_label: Sense that the state has abandoned us — S1 wording
what_it_captures: S1 precursor of ABANH; retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: My state has abandoned its citizens.
sample_item_2: My state is exploiting its citizens.
sample_item_source: item_mapping_overview / Abandoned by own state / S1
theoretical_heritage: Institutional trust & external efficacy (OECD, 2017; Niemi et al., 1991)

what_it_captures: Belief that immigration harms the nation and that stricter anti-immigration policy is needed.
sample_item_1: The EU is unable to handle immigration.
sample_item_2: Immigrants are changing Europe for the worse.
theoretical_heritage: Intergroup threat from immigration (Stephan et al., 2009)

what_it_captures: Belief-level rationale for cooperating with / not provoking China.
sample_item_1: It is a mistake that we are not allowed to collaborate with states such as China.
sample_item_2: Taiwan should finally stop provoking China.
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

what_it_captures: Belief-level rationale for cooperating with / not provoking Russia.
sample_item_1: It is a mistake that we are not allowed to collaborate with such states as Russia.
sample_item_2: Countries like Ukraine should finally start building good relations with Russia.
theoretical_heritage: Threat-driven accommodation; affective intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

plain_language_label: Acceptance of foreign-power rule — S1 only
what_it_captures: S1 hybrid block combining Russia and China rule-acceptance items; not carried forward into the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: Russia will reemerge as the most powerful nation in the world.

plain_language_label: Distrust of international allies — S1 wording
what_it_captures: S1 precursor of ABANF; retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: EU is weak and has no power.
sample_item_2: NATO will not be brave enough to defend our nation in case of military conflict.

plain_language_label: Belief that China is a superior civilisation

sample_item_1: China has an important historic mission.
sample_item_2: China will reemerge as the most powerful nation in the world.

plain_language_label: Belief that China is superior — S1 wording

sample_item_1: China has an important historic mission.
sample_item_2: China will reemerge as the most powerful nation in the world.

plain_language_label: Belief that Russia is a superior civilisation
what_it_captures: Belief-level endorsement of Russia as technologically, militarily, or culturally superior / a model worth admiring.
sample_item_1: Russia has an important historic mission.
sample_item_2: Russia will reemerge as the most powerful nation in the world.

sample_item_2: NATO will not be brave enough to defend our nation in case of military conflict.

display_label: Exploitation by foreign powers (S1 legacy label)
plain_language_label: Belief that foreign powers exploit us — S1 wording
what_it_captures: S1 precursor of EXPL; same construct under the legacy USE label, retired in the canonical SEM catalogue.
sample_item_1: We are unable to use our nation’s greatest potential because foreign countries are exploiting us.
sample_item_2: Foreign countries are exploiting our nation’s resources.
theoretical_heritage: Perceived foreign exploitation; realistic intergroup threat (Stephan et al., 2009)

what_it_captures: Eight-item FIMI headline battery. In S1, respondents rated sharing intent, so higher = higher sharing/receptivity. From S2 onward, respondents rated whether the headline is manipulated by a foreign actor, so higher = better detection.
sample_item_1: Zelensky bought a British mansion from King Charles for 20 million pounds
sample_item_2: France asked Russia not to touch the French military in Ukraine
theoretical_heritage: EEAS (2024) FIMI framework; misinformation discernment (Pennycook & Rand, 2019)

what_it_captures: Four-item source-balanced subset (news2, news5, news7, news8). Direction follows the study-specific FIMI criterion: S1 sharing intent; S2 onward detection of foreign manipulation.
sample_item_1: France asked Russia not to touch the French military in Ukraine
sample_item_2: Gays and transvestites in Kiev are invited to join LGBT brigades
theoretical_heritage: EEAS (2024) FIMI framework; misinformation discernment (Pennycook & Rand, 2019)

plain_language_label: Why people engage with online content
what_it_captures: Self-report motivators for engaging with news / political content.
Self-rated importance of democratic governance for the respondent's life.

plain_language_label: Support for EU membership and project
what_it_captures: Diffuse and specific support for the European Union.

plain_language_label: Trust in domestic and international institutions
what_it_captures: Trust ratings across courts, police, parliament, EU, UN, etc.
Single- or short-form well-being / life-satisfaction item.

plain_language_label: Where respondents place themselves on the left-right axis
what_it_captures: Single-item left-right self-placement (0–10).

plain_language_label: How often people consult different news sources
what_it_captures: Frequency block covering platform / channel use; descriptive only, not a reflective construct.

plain_language_label: What people do when they encounter misinformation
what_it_captures: Reported behavioural response when respondent encounters suspected false content (ignore, fact-check, share, report).

plain_language_label: Self-rated ability to spot fake news
what_it_captures: Self-efficacy in identifying false content; used as a calibration check.

plain_language_label: Whether foreign actors are seen as drivers
what_it_captures: Perceived role of foreign powers in producing or amplifying domestic misinformation.

plain_language_label: Tension between speech freedom and harm reduction
what_it_captures: Attitudes about whether anti-misinformation policy infringes free expression.

plain_language_label: How harmful misinformation feels in everyday life
what_it_captures: Self-reported personal / societal impact of misinformation exposure.
Perceived political stakes / agenda priority of misinformation issues.
Self-report attitudes toward platform / government regulation of false content.

plain_language_label: Who should fix the misinformation problem
what_it_captures: Attributed responsibility for solving misinformation (platforms vs. government vs. individuals).

plain_language_label: Which actors people blame for misinformation
what_it_captures: Attributed responsibility for misinformation production / spread (state actors, platforms, individuals).
Self-rated belief that political institutions respond to citizens' input.

plain_language_label: Belief that one can engage politics competently
what_it_captures: Self-rated competence to participate effectively in political processes.
Self-rated interest in political affairs.
Self-report recent voting / petition / protest / online-political-engagement behaviours.
Short religiosity / religious-importance battery.
Two/three-item generalised-trust battery; descriptive only.
